chapters.title: Two sides of the same coin
chapters.text:
In the long history of North America, one ship played a role that often slips past us. Most people think of the Mayflower, which brought the British Pilgrims to this land in 1620. A year before that, another ship reached the Virginia colony. It marked the start of an era that was also one of the most tragic.

So what happened in 1619. In August, a ship called The White Lion came to the shores of America carrying several dozen captive Africans. They were sold to the people of Virginia and were the first of the millions who would be considered enslaved on this land over the next 250 years.

Before World War II, this forced migration stood as the largest in history.

Enslaved people brought knowledge of growing crops, treating rare diseases, and crafting goods. With their labor, they built the economy and the institutions that propped up the young American nation and its democracy. Yet the authorities kept ignoring the rights of these people, who made up one fifth of the country's population.

While white men, and only white men at first, since women had almost no rights then, made choices about their homes, careers, and presidents, Black people were not even treated as owners of their own lives. They stayed powerless and invisible on the map of a country they had built with their own hands.

The 1619 Project became a meaningful investigation for its author as well. Since childhood, Nikole Hannah-Jones had come across success stories that featured the achievements of white people. She did not know what her own ancestors had done to develop the American economy, politics, and social life. The sources she studied showed her that many generations of Black people had been the foundation of that growth, and they had fought on another front for their own freedom and equality.

This summary looks at events from the era of slavery and after its abolition, and at their impact on modern American society. There is no sugarcoating. Each section offers clear facts so you can draw your own conclusions.

A 2019 Washington Post poll showed that most Americans know very little about the history of slavery, and that they do not have enough knowledge to share with their own children, who grow up without a strong reason to study the topic.

chapters.title: Where did the idea of race come from
chapters.text:
People like to sort their lives into categories to feel in control. Buying a house fits in the category of big expenses, and helping another person fits in the category of good deeds. There is one important caveat though. A sense of control should not come from putting labels on other people. It is wrong to judge the value of a human life by placing someone in a category and comparing them with others. A few centuries ago, that principle was not so obvious.

The sorting of people by race began in the era of slavery. It had calculated political and economic reasons behind it. When a white man had relations with a black woman, it was hard to set the status of their children. Would they get rights like their father or remain in the position of their mother. The rules that were adopted took the second path. Newborn children inherited the race of their mothers and stayed enslaved, which let the enslavers grow their pool of free labor. Black women, who could not protect themselves, their bodies, or their children, were in the worst position of all.

Several centuries later, people still run into the urge to assign everyone to their place.

Dr. Walter Ashby Plecker was one of the people behind the Racial Integrity Act of 1924, which banned miscegenation and required racial identity to be verified. Plecker pulled together a group of doctors, government clerks, and even undertakers to enforce the law. How could such a document exist in the twentieth century. These were echoes of the colonial era. At that time, gender and race still played a decisive role in many areas of life. The act was finally struck down in 1967, when the US Supreme Court ruled it unconstitutional.

We can still feel the influence of such laws today. When we fill out certain forms and see a line asking what race we belong to, it is fair to ask why that information is collected and how it will be used, for statistics or for sorting people into boxes.

chapters.title: Fear turns violent
chapters.text:
How can someone want something they have never known. How can people who were not born free reach for freedom in a place it was never meant for them. Their longing shows that freedom has always been a universal value, one that should be open to everyone. Enslavers did not draw that conclusion. They did not think they were violating human rights. They only feared losing control of Black people one day, and they feared that any freedom might let enslaved people take revenge.

In 1669, a law in the Carolina colony gave every white man unlimited power over the people he held captive. They could be sold to other enslavers, forced to work long hours, and poorly fed. What cut deeper was that they could be punished in any way for the smallest mistake. White people controlled the people they called their property and tried to shield themselves from possible uprisings. Their fear grew so large that in the first decades of the 1700s, they set up patrols to keep order and chase down rumors of rebellion.

There was a fine of 40 shillings for failing to keep proper control over the activities of Black people.

Even so, enslaved people sometimes managed to push back against the injustice directed at them. Those actions were always met with a violent reaction.

In 1739 in South Carolina, a few dozen enslaved Africans attacked the plantations, killed enslavers, and invited others to join them. All the rebels were tracked down and publicly executed. The following year, a new act required enslaved people to obey the orders of all enslavers. Black people were also forbidden from leaving their workplaces alone or meeting in groups of more than three.

They were treated as potential criminals and rule breakers by white people. Enslaved people were punished for what they had not done in order to prevent what they might do. Even that could not kill their desire for freedom.

That line about liberty or death comes from the writer James Baldwin.

chapters.title: Citizenship gives freedom a voice
chapters.text:
People gain more rights and opportunities when the country they live in recognizes them as citizens. That sounds obvious. History shows, though, that people who helped build a new state could be treated as if they did not exist. Black people would have a sense of belonging, justice, and safety if they were accepted as full members of American society.

The first documented record of citizenship comes from Ancient Greece. There, only free adult men could receive the official status of a city citizen.

Until the 1820s, the American government could not decide what status enslaved people should hold. Workers in state institutions were haunted by the US Constitution, which said that all people are equal. Black activists kept reminding them of that line and pushed back against segregation and for equal rights with other Americans.

The most important day in this story is January 1, 1863, when Abraham Lincoln issued the Emancipation Proclamation. On that day, the sun of hope rose for enslaved people. They heard from the president that they were being given freedom. Obtaining citizenship became more real in 1868 when a new amendment to the Constitution declared birthright citizenship. That allowed Black people to become US citizens from birth, to count on legal protection, and to vote.

Congress set up the Freedmen's Bureau to take care of newly emancipated citizens. The bureau found housing, guaranteed food and medical care, and looked for grants to fund schools for Black people.

To support them, authorities in some states organized groups that kept order in the streets and could protect Black people when needed. Notice the shift. A hundred and fifty years earlier, the police had protected white people from attacks by enslaved groups. Now, newly emancipated citizens could need protection from the aggression of white people.

At the same time, it is worth knowing that these were the first steps toward equality and the end of racism. In the 1870s, Jim Crow laws began to spread across the South. Their purpose was to strip freed people of their rights and chances to grow. These harsh laws kept appearing for decades, sometimes allowing Black people a small measure of political power and sometimes taking it away.

chapters.title: Where was God in all of this
chapters.text:
Yes, you read the title right. It is time to talk about God, and about where God was when Black people were enslaved and their lives were reduced to nothing.

When enslaved Africans were first brought to America, only their oral culture and beliefs traveled with them. White people called them pagans and used that label to justify cruelty. Over time, cut off from their roots, Black people lost their traditions. They converted to Christianity and baptized their children.

How could they become Christians and still be enslaved. Enslavers adopted local laws to smooth over that gap, which let the church keep teaching Christianity while ignoring the enslaved status of its members.

The spread of the gospels, the rise of Black preachers, and access to a basic education gave people room to think about freedom and mutual aid. When they had a chance to gather in church communities, they used those meetings to speak out about the open hypocrisy of Americans. The absurdity made them angry. People who called themselves Christians had enslaved and stripped of all rights those who believed in the same God.

In the 19th century, two of the most influential Black rights speakers were the preacher James H. Cone and Martin Luther King, Jr.

People created the first political organizations out of their religious communities. The churches of newly emancipated citizens fell under an old and familiar fear of white people, who worried about a seizure of power, unrest, and violence. A law can decree that all people become free and equal in rights from a given day, but it takes a great deal of effort and time for them to agree and sincerely believe in such ideas.

Over the following decades, the church became a place with calls for white people to be forgiven, demands for retribution, and the spread of Black nationalism. Worn down by deep disappointment, newly emancipated people tried to recover feelings of compassion and love through faith.

chapters.title: From fake science to poor medical care
chapters.text:
In the history of slavery, many crimes committed by white people against Black people went unpunished. Among them were the studies done by doctors and professors on the bodies of enslaved people. The bodies were used for surgery practice or for trying out new treatments. Under the label of research, doctors put forward "discoveries" about Black bodies. These were made up claims that no one checked.

In 1787, Dr. Benjamin Moseley argued in a treatise, based on subjective assumptions, that Black people can endure pain more easily than white people.

These "scientific" conclusions fed racist theories about deep differences in the bodies of Black and white people. Their harmful effects lingered long after the Civil War. Beyond the struggle for food, newly emancipated people also suffered from poor medical care. Doctors knew that unsanitary conditions and missing vaccinations were unacceptable, but racist stereotypes led them to believe that Black people needed less care, so they treated white patients first.

Access to treatment began to shift when the Freedmen's Bureau opened its Medical Division to give new citizens access to hospitals and consultations. In 1895, the National Medical Association, or NMA, was founded as the first organization of Black doctors. Their work drew the attention of authorities to health issues, helped fund medical institutions for Black people, and supported campaigns on hygiene. Members of the NMA also pushed for the idea of a public health insurance system open to every citizen.

Even so, the United States still cannot offer equal access to health care for all its people. The covid pandemic made that clear. During the crisis, many Black people in densely populated neighborhoods, who struggled with health insurance, could not count on doctors' services.

It is fair to admit that status, country of origin, and race still play a serious role in parts of modern American life. The sooner this is acknowledged by the authorities and the public, the sooner real change can begin.

chapters.title: Music as a shared inheritance
chapters.text:
The one thing white people envied was the music of Black people. Without those musical traditions, the world would never have heard the blues, jazz, and rock and roll. Before the first captive Africans set foot on American soil in 1619, white people had never heard melodies and songs quite like those.

During work on the plantations, during building projects, and at evening gatherings around the fire, enslaved people sang songs they had brought from home. They passed oral stories from one to another, created ballads, and stayed connected while they worked. It was their main way of making their lives sound and heard.

For a long time, Black people were not allowed to use musical instruments. The music came only from their voices and from the sounds of nature around them.

As Black people became more familiar with Christianity, they began to weave biblical stories into their songs. These spirituals caught the attention of white listeners, who wanted access to the music. They started writing down the melodies so they could be reproduced. In 1867, the first collection called Slave Songs of the United States was published.

Music also gave newly emancipated people a way to share space with white people, though those spaces were mostly dance floors or halls. The former worked and played instruments after their main jobs, while the latter rested and had fun.

This is an important point. Black music was treated as entertainment, plain and simple. Because of that, the careers of Louis Armstrong, Ella Fitzgerald, Nina Simone, and other brilliant performers took a long time to be recognized. The understanding that their lyrics, performance, and instrumental work were unique only came with the broader recognition that emancipated people deserved respect and the right to grow professionally.

chapters.title: Moments that shift how people think
chapters.text:
On February 1, 1960, four Black students from North Carolina Agricultural and Technical State University walked up to a lunch counter. They stopped near a Whites Only sign and ordered coffee. Nobody was going to fill their order, but the students stayed by the counter for the entire day.

The 1619 Project includes a short story about that day, written by the American novelist Terry McMillan. Her piece From Behind the Counter paints a vivid picture of what the counter clerk felt as he watched the four students.

The clerk, like his visitors, was Black. He panicked when he saw the students move toward the Whites Only sign. He ignored their coffee order and did not look them in the eye at first. He could not understand how such an act was even possible.

On the other hand, who had put that sign there and why. Who decided that Black and white people could not eat at the same table. The clerk did not have answers to those questions, but he felt sure that something had shifted in his own mind. Once the counter emptied, he poured himself a coffee and sat down by the sign.

The four students were taken away by the police. They came back the next day, and with them, people in other states began sit-ins at lunch counters across the South.

Remember the names of those four young people. David Richmond, Franklin McCain, Joseph McNeil, and Ezell Blair. They were among the brave souls who stepped up to show others that things could be different. Over the years, the number of such stories has only grown. By the summer of 1960, many dining areas in the southern states had become accessible to everyone, regardless of race.

chapters.title: Closing thoughts
chapters.text:
From 1619 to 1863, dark times held North America in their grip. For several hundred years, this land was shaped by segregation, twisted facts, and cruel treatment of a particular group of people. That past cannot be erased from memory or pulled out of history books. Citizens still feel its traces in the daily life of a country that claims to stand for democracy and freedom.

It does no good to avoid discussions about slavery. People need to recognize that era as a great wound to humanity and to accept their duty to Black people. Their service to the American nation cannot be overstated. Their work, persistence, cultural heritage, and fight against injustice shaped the development of the United States.

Over that long stretch, newly emancipated people understood that they would often stand alone in their efforts to claim their rights. They had to take active steps on their own to make their presence in society visible. Even today, Black people know that expanding their rights and access to services can also bring unfair reactions from others.

The time has come to show them that they are not alone in their struggle for freedom, equality, and a real place in the life of the nation. We cannot change the past, but the future is open to change. It can be genuinely bright for everyone if each citizen chooses action over silence and refuses to let old mistakes repeat.

That line about how we think about history is from Nikole Hannah-Jones.

Try this. Share what you have learned with friends or family. Talk about which facts surprised or upset you, and which you already knew. Discuss your feelings and thoughts out loud. Attend events that take place every year during Black History Month. That kind of self education and contact with other activists helps the work continue.
